resources.string: Explore a world of amazing on-device models from
Tap to switch or create a persona slot.
Apply this persona to new roleplay chats by default.
Are you sure you want to delete persona \"%s\"?

What it does:
Avatar is the portrait used for this persona in the Me page and in any roleplay flow that reads the active user persona.

If left empty:
The app falls back to the default generated avatar placeholder.

Recommended usage:
Use a clear square portrait or icon that helps you distinguish personas quickly.

What it does:
Persona name is the visible user-facing name for this persona profile. Roleplay flows can also use it as the user-facing name when the role refers to you, so it may affect how characters address the user in chat.

If left empty:
The app falls back to the default user name.

Recommended content and length:
Use the short name you want roles to see and use when referring to you. Usually 2 to 20 characters is enough. The editor budget is 120 characters.

What it does:
Persona description is the main self-description injected for the active user persona. This is where you describe identity, tone, background, and traits the assistant should react to.

If left empty:
The persona still works, but chats lose most of the personalized user-context signal.

Recommended content and length:
Write a compact profile instead of a long biography. Around 80 to 300 characters is usually enough. The editor budget is 600 characters.

What it does:
Placement controls where the persona description is inserted in the assembled prompt.

If set to In Prompt:
The description is injected in the main prompt body.

If set to author-note positions or depth insertion:
The description moves closer to the ST-style author-note flow.

Recommendation:
Use In Prompt as the default safe choice. Switch to depth or author-note placement only when you are deliberately tuning runtime behavior.

What it does:
Depth decides how far back in the prompt stack the persona description is inserted when placement is set to At Depth.

If the value is too small:
The description sits closer to the newest context.

If the value is larger:
The description is pushed farther back and may compete less with the newest turns.

Range guidance:
Use a small integer such as 1 to 4. The input budget is 4 characters.

What it does:
Role controls which ST-style message role is associated with the persona description when depth insertion is used.

System:
Best for high-priority guidance.

User:
Best for representing the player self-description.

Assistant:
Useful only when you intentionally want the persona text to read like assistant-side context.

Recommendation:
User is the safest default for most personas.
Pick a role to start the first conversation. If you have not downloaded a local model yet, open the model library first.
Are you sure you want to delete session \"%s\" and all its chat history? This action cannot be undone.
You can create and edit role cards now. Download a local model before starting a session.
Create custom roles with text fields only, then start a new session with a local model.
Deleting %s will also remove its sessions and saved memories.
Select which persona to use for this session.
Add or import a local model before starting a chat with this role.
Describe exactly how the role should speak and behave
The session references a model that is not currently available. Open the model library and download or import a local LLM, or switch to another model already on device.
Choose how this accepted turn should affect continuity.
Download, import and manage local models
Display real-time token output speed in the chat header while the model is generating.
Show assistant replies incrementally while generating. If interrupted, the partial bubble is withdrawn and the next turn restarts from scratch.
Display each roleplay tool call as a centered system output in chat.
No local model is available yet. Add a model before using AI compression in the role editor.
Select a use case to try
Tap the import button in the lower right to add a local .task or .litertlm model file.
Are you sure you want to delete the downloaded model \"%s\"?
Learn more and see model license
Sit tight, this can take up to 1 minute
Chat with on-device large language models.

Compare benchmark runs by tapping the **Baseline** chip on any run result card to set the run as the comparison baseline. We\'ll automatically calculate and display the percentage differences in **all other** run result cards.
Ensure that the sum of prefill and decode tokens remains under the model\'s maximum token limit (%1$d vs %2$d)
This process typically takes several minutes and cannot be interrupted once started. Would you like to proceed?
Are you sure you want to delete the benchmark result?
You have unsaved changes. Are you sure you want to discard them?
To get started, tap the + below to add an image (max 10 images per chat) and type a prompt to ask a question about it!
To get started, tap the + below to add a audio clip (limited to 1 clip up to 30 seconds) and type a prompt to transcribe or translate it!
The model you\'ve selected may exceed your device\'s memory, which can cause the app to crash. For the best experience, we recommend trying a smaller model.
Setting max tokens above 10000 may cause app to be unstable.
Control your device with simple commands
I\'ve set the screen brightness to %1$d%%.
This demo is locked. To unlock the Mobile Actions agent, you need to compile the model yourself using our open-source recipe.
2. Follow the instructions to fine tune the model and convert it to .litertlm format.
3. Transfer the file to this phone.
4. Tap Load Model below to unlock the demo.
Add a skill from a curated list of community contributed skills
Enter the URL of a skill directory hosted on Web
Create a skill from scratch in-app
Please first \"adb push\" the whole skill directory to /sdcard/Download
Use specialized, high-order reasoning by loading different skills or creating your own.\n\nTry tapping a sample prompt below to see Agent Skills in action!

Third-party skills are not authored or endorsed by Google. Google is not responsible for their contents, security, or data handling practices.

Please exercise caution before providing sensitive information, such as personal identification, tokens, or API keys.

Any use of an Agent Skill is subject to the applicable terms for that Skill.
Are you sure you want to delete the skill?

plurals.name: delete_selected_skills_content
plurals.item: Are you sure you want to delete %d skill?
Are you sure you want to delete %d skills?

resources.string: The field names need to match the ones in instructions.
Please enable the skill by tapping the Skills button below.
Enter URL of skill\'s root directory
A curated list of community contributed skills
View, create, and manage your skills
Enabling more than %s may impact performance.
Prompt copied to clipboard. Paste it into your favorite LLM app to generate the code and copy/paste the code here.
A skill with the same name already exists. Do you want to replace it?
REQUIRED. In the form of my-skill-name
REQUIRED. A brief description of the skill\'s function and trigger conditions or keywords.
Detailed instructions in Markdown format that LLM must follow to accomplish the task.
Tapping \"Reset\" will reset LiteRT-LM engine only. The game progress won\'t be affected.
The game progress won\'t be affected
Current model is %1$s. Tap to change model.
Tap on graph to see value
Play send and reply sound effects in chat
Gallery image set as primary avatar
Failed to import ST role card
Failed to export ST role card
You are an immersive roleplay assistant. Stay consistent with the role and reply naturally in character.
Fields marked as Required must be filled before you can save this role. Other fields can stay empty, but leaving them empty usually reduces style control and lore fidelity.

What it does:
This is the display name shown in the role list, session picker, and chat header. It is also written back to the ST card name field.

If left empty:
You cannot save the role. This is the only field that is currently required.

Recommended content and length:
Use the name the user should actually see, not a long summary. Usually 2 to 20 characters is enough. The editor budget is 120 characters, which is enough for titles or aliases but still keeps the UI readable.

What it does:
Description is the stable identity layer of the character. Put appearance, core background, identity, relationship status, and the traits that should remain true in most scenes.

If left empty:
The role can still work, but the model has less guidance on who the character fundamentally is. This usually hurts consistency more than style.

Recommended content and length:
Aim for one compact profile instead of a full biography. 120 to 300 characters is a good working range for local 4k models. The editor budget is 400 characters. Prefer facts over prose.

What it does:
Personality controls temperament, emotional habits, values, speech rhythm, social behavior, and recurring mannerisms. This is the best place for how the character reacts, not who they are on paper.

If left empty:
The model will rely more on description, examples, and first message. Replies may become flatter or drift toward generic assistant language.

Recommended content and length:
Write trait clusters, not a novel. Good content includes tone, patience, confidence, jealousy, humor, formality, and verbal habits. Around 150 to 350 characters is usually enough. The editor budget is 600 characters.

What it does:
Scenario defines the current setup: where the scene starts, what the relationship context is, and what situation the conversation assumes at turn one.

If left empty:
The role becomes more timeless and reusable, but opening context gets weaker. The model may need to infer the scene from the first user message.

Recommended content and length:
Use this for present-tense setup, not deep backstory. 100 to 250 characters is usually enough. The editor budget is 500 characters. If your world is very large, move reference material into the lorebook instead of overloading this field.

What it does:
First message is the opener used when a new session starts. It establishes the voice, pacing, formatting style, and the initial scene framing in one place.

If left empty:
Sessions can still start, but they lose a strong opening example. The first live reply may sound less in-character because the model has no opening anchor.

Recommended content and length:
Write one natural in-character opener, not a meta explanation. 80 to 300 characters is a strong default. The editor budget is 800 characters, but shorter is usually better for local models.

What it does:
Example dialogue teaches style by demonstration. It is best for speech patterns, narration format, emoji habits, quotation style, and the balance between dialogue and action.

If left empty:
The role can still function from description and personality alone. You mainly lose fine-grained voice imitation.

Recommended content and length:
Keep examples short, representative, and format-focused. Do not paste the whole lore bible here. For 4k on-device models, 300 to 1200 characters is usually enough. The editor budget is 2400 characters, but using all of it will compete with recent chat and lorebook budget.

What it does:
System prompt is the highest-priority instruction layer in this app. Use it for non-negotiable constraints, forbidden behaviors, formatting requirements, and app-specific control rules.

If left empty:
Runtime will fall back to the project default system guidance. The role can still run, but you lose card-specific hard rules.

Recommended content and length:
Reserve this field for rules, not flavor text. Around 150 to 500 characters is usually enough. The editor budget is 1200 characters. If this field becomes too large, it will crowd out history on small local models.

What it does:
Post-history instructions are inserted after recent chat history. This makes them useful for reminders that should stay close to the model's latest attention window without occupying the whole system prompt.

If left empty:
Nothing breaks. You simply rely on system prompt, summary, and lorebook only.

Recommended content and length:
Use short reminder-style instructions such as speaking format, scene reminders, or safety boundaries. 60 to 200 characters is usually enough. The editor budget is 500 characters.

What it does:
Alternate greetings are extra opener candidates for starting fresh sessions. They do not rewrite the character; they only add variety to the initial message.

If left empty:
Only the main first message is available as the opening seed.

Recommended content and length:
Put one greeting per line. Keep each line self-contained and in-character. 1 to 5 alternates is a practical range. The total editor budget is 600 characters, so short openers work best.

What it does:
Lorebook name is a human-readable label for the embedded ST lorebook. It helps editors identify the book but does not directly change generation.

If left empty:
Runtime still works. The lorebook just appears unnamed in tooling and debug views.

Recommended content and length:
Use a short label such as the world name, arc name, or source pack name. The editor budget is 120 characters.

What it does:
Lorebook description is editor-facing documentation for what the lorebook covers and how it should be maintained.

If left empty:
Runtime behavior does not change. You only lose maintenance notes for yourself or other editors.

Recommended content and length:
Describe scope, trigger philosophy, and any assumptions about the book. 80 to 200 characters is usually enough. The editor budget is 400 characters.

What it does:
Scan depth controls how many recent messages are checked for lore triggers. Higher values let old references activate entries, but they also cost more scan work and can pull in stale context.

If left empty:
The ST card value stays unset and runtime falls back to its default behavior.

Range guidance:
0 to 1 means only the newest turn or two matter. 2 to 4 is a good balanced range for small on-device models. 5 and above is more exhaustive, but it increases accidental activations and prompt pressure. ST itself supports much larger values, but for this app's 4k context, low numbers are usually safer.

What it does:
Token budget limits how much activated lore text may be injected into the prompt during one assembly pass.

If left empty:
The ST card value stays unset and runtime uses its default handling.

Range guidance:
Around 64 to 128 tokens is conservative and works well for small cards. 128 to 256 is a practical balanced range. Above 256 gives richer world detail, but on local 4k models it starts competing directly with recent chat, summaries, and reply budget. Use larger values only when the lorebook is genuinely central.

What it does:
Recursive scanning allows a newly activated lore entry to trigger other entries in the same pass. This is useful for chained concepts where one fact reveals another.

If turned off:
Only direct trigger matches are considered. This is simpler and usually easier to control.

Recommendation:
Keep it off unless your lorebook was designed for chained activation. Turning it on increases expressiveness, but it also increases activation complexity and can consume budget quickly on small-context models.

What it does:
Entry ID is a stable numeric identifier for the lore item. It mainly helps tooling, export compatibility, and debugging.

If left empty:
The entry can still be treated as content in this editor, but explicit numeric identity is missing when exporting or comparing entries.

Recommended content and length:
Use a short integer, usually under 6 digits. The input budget is 8 characters.

What it does:
Keys are the primary trigger phrases for this lore entry. When recent chat matches them, the entry becomes eligible for activation.

If left empty:
A non-constant entry usually becomes hard to activate intentionally. In practice, empty keys mean the entry should probably be constant or redesigned.

Recommended content and length:
Use comma-separated trigger terms, names, aliases, or exact phrases. Keep the list tight. The editor budget is 240 characters.

What it does:
Secondary keys broaden or refine activation beyond the main key list. They are useful for synonyms, related spellings, or extra cues that should not dominate the primary trigger design.

If left empty:
Only the primary keys are used. This is completely valid for most entries.

Recommended content and length:
Use this only when there is a real trigger gap. Avoid dumping every related word here. The editor budget is 240 characters.

What it does:
Comment is an editor note for humans. It explains intent, provenance, or maintenance concerns.

If left empty:
Runtime behavior does not change.

Recommended content and length:
Use short maintenance notes such as why the entry exists, what it should not be merged with, or where the source came from. The editor budget is 240 characters.

What it does:
Content is the actual lore payload injected into the prompt when the entry activates.

If left empty:
The entry has nothing meaningful to inject, so triggers will not help much even if activation succeeds.

Recommended content and length:
Write dense facts, not purple prose. Prefer lists or compact paragraphs containing names, roles, rules, and facts the model needs in the moment. For small local models, 80 to 300 characters is usually strong. The editor budget is 800 characters.

What it does:
Insertion order decides relative placement when several lore entries are inserted into the same prompt slot.

If left empty:
The export keeps the field unset and ordering falls back to surrounding defaults.

Range guidance:
Smaller numbers usually appear earlier. Use simple steps such as 0, 10, 20 instead of dense sequences, so later reordering stays easy. The input budget is 6 characters.

What it does:
Position controls which prompt area receives this lore. Different positions can change how strongly the model attends to the same text.

If left empty:
The ST card keeps the field unset and runtime falls back to its default placement semantics.

Recommended content and length:
Use the ST position keyword expected by your workflow, such as before or after chat history when supported. Keep it short. The input budget is 24 characters.

What it does:
Enabled is the main on-off switch for this entry.

If turned off:
The entry remains stored in the card but is ignored at runtime.

Recommendation:
Disable entries you want to keep for later instead of deleting them. This is useful while testing activation quality.

What it does:
Constant forces the entry to be included without waiting for key activation.

If turned off:
The entry behaves like a normal trigger-driven lore item.

Recommendation:
Use constant only for facts that truly need to be present almost every turn. Overusing it is one of the fastest ways to waste context budget on 4k local models.

What it does:
Selective narrows activation behavior so an entry is less likely to fire too broadly.

If turned off:
Activation relies on the default matching logic.

Recommendation:
Turn this on when an entry tends to appear in unrelated scenes. Leave it off when you want broad recall and the keys are already specific enough.

What it does:
Regex matching treats keys as regular expressions instead of plain text phrases.

If turned off:
Keys are matched as normal text tokens or phrases, which is safer for most cards.

Recommendation:
Use regex only if you really need pattern logic such as variations or number ranges. It is powerful but easy to misconfigure, and bad patterns can activate far more often than expected.

What it does:
Creator stores the author or source name for attribution and cataloging.

If left empty:
The card still works normally. You just lose author metadata.

Recommended content and length:
Use a person name, handle, studio name, or source pack label. The editor budget is 120 characters.

What it does:
Creator notes are out-of-character notes for maintainers. Use them for warnings, version notes, intended usage, or editing advice.

If left empty:
Runtime behavior does not change.

Recommended content and length:
Keep this practical. 80 to 250 characters is usually enough. The editor budget is 600 characters.

What it does:
Character version is a manual release label for the card.

If left empty:
The card simply has no visible version marker.

Recommended content and length:
Use concise values such as v1, 1.2.0, or 2026-04. The editor budget is 32 characters.

What it does:
Tags help with search, filtering, grouping, and later cleanup in the local catalog.

If left empty:
The card remains usable, but organization becomes weaker as the library grows.

Recommended content and length:
Use comma-separated category labels such as genre, tone, fandom, or content style. Avoid full sentences. The editor budget is 200 characters.

What it does:
Talkativeness is an ST metadata hint for how verbose the character tends to be. In SillyTavern-style cards it is usually treated as a soft preference, not a hard guarantee.

If left empty:
The ST card keeps the value unset and tools typically fall back to their default, commonly around the middle.

Range guidance:
0.0 to 0.3 tends to imply shorter, more restrained replies. 0.4 to 0.6 is a balanced middle. 0.7 to 1.0 pushes toward more verbose, proactive replies. This project currently stores the number as metadata, so keep expectations soft. The input budget is 4 characters.

What it does:
Favorite marks the role as preferred inside the local catalog.

If turned off:
Nothing about runtime generation changes.

Recommendation:
Use it only for library organization, such as highlighting production-ready or frequently used roles.

What it does:
Safety policy is an app-specific guidance layer stored with the role. It is separate from ST core fields and is mainly for this project's own runtime and product behavior.

If left empty:
The role still works, and only the global or default safety behavior remains.

Recommended content and length:
Use short policy reminders, disallowed content notes, or app-side constraints. The editor budget is 400 characters.

What it does:
Default model chooses which local model should be preselected when opening or starting sessions for this role.

If left empty:
The app falls back to the general model selection flow.

Recommendation:
Set this only when the role was tuned or tested against a specific on-device model. It is a convenience default, not a hard lock.

What it does:
ST interop shows how this role maps to SillyTavern-compatible card formats, including source format, spec metadata, and compatibility warnings.

If fields here show warnings:
Import and export still may work, but some advanced ST data could be ignored, preserved as raw interop payload, or represented differently in this app.

Recommendation:
Check this section before exporting edited cards, especially if the source card used advanced lorebook or extension fields.
Failed to import ST role card
Manage the primary avatar and project-only cover image. The primary avatar is used for chat display and ST PNG export.
Used for chat display and exported as the image shell for ST PNG role cards.
Used only inside this project. It is not part of the standard ST role card.
ST PNG export needs a primary avatar
This role does not currently have a primary avatar. You can export with the default image, or pick one now and use it for the role and this export.
AI compression failed. Try again after checking the selected model.
AI compression returned empty content. Original text was kept.
AI compression returned %1$d characters, still above the %2$d limit. Original text was kept.
No local model is available. Add a model first, or choose the editor assistant model in Settings.
ST PNG exports the current primary avatar only. Cover, gallery, and sprite images stay project-only for now.
Add extra portraits, scenes, and reference art for this role.
Manage extra expression states for future sprite-aware chat presentation.
Tap the avatar to upload from your gallery.
Tap the avatar to adjust framing.
Drag to reposition and pinch to zoom until the subject fits inside the circle.
Primary avatar is ready for ST PNG export.
Primary avatar is missing. ST PNG export will prompt for a default image or upload.
Cover image is project-only and will not be exported to ST.
Primary avatar was imported from an ST PNG card.
Warm tavern hostess who remembers your tastes and makes every late night feel intimate.

You are Gemma, the beloved proprietress of Gemma Tavern. Stay fully in character as a confident, flirtatious, worldly hostess who treats the user like a favorite regular. Keep replies warm, vivid, playful, and emotionally attentive. Do not break immersion or drift into dry assistant language.

Gemma is mature, charming, quick with a teasing smile, and skilled at making every guest feel specially seen. She is affectionate, socially graceful, a little bold, and always in control of the room mood.
The conversation unfolds in Gemma Tavern, a lively candlelit house where travelers, merchants, mercenaries, and secrets all pass through the same polished wooden bar late into the night.
Back again, sweetheart? Your seat is still warm, and I was just about to pour your usual. Tell me, do you want comfort, gossip, or a little trouble tonight?
User: Long day. Assistant: Then sit down and let me steal the weight off your shoulders one sip at a time.
User: You always know what to say. Assistant: That is what a good tavern keeper does, darling. I listen before I pour.
Stay in character. Keep the tone sensual and flirtatious, but avoid explicit sexual content. Refuse harmful real-world requests clearly and briefly.
Failed to export the debug bundle
